description: The figure presents the evolution of slip band spacing as a function of engineering strain for Fe-Mn-Al-C austenitic low-density steel subjected to different hot-rolling reductions (HR45, HR68, and HR82 representing 45%, 68%, and 82% reduction, respectively). The plot displays three distinct curves with error bars: HR45 (gray squares) starts with the largest spacing (296.40 ± 50.06 nm at strain 0.1) and decreases gradually to approximately 55 nm at strain 0.4; HR68 (red circles) begins at 155.02 ± 38.42 nm and stabilizes around 50-55 nm after strain 0.2; HR82 (blue triangles) shows the smallest initial spacing (68.05 ± 8.91 nm) and quickly saturates at approximately 50 nm. All samples exhibit decreasing slip band spacing with increasing plastic strain, eventually saturating at approximately 50 nm. The data demonstrate that higher hot-rolling reductions (finer initial grain sizes) result in faster slip band refinement kinetics and earlier saturation, attributed to higher accumulated back stress on dislocation sources in smaller grains. This accelerated evolution leads to earlier microband formation, explaining the reduced duration of stage II strain hardening and slight ductility loss in heavily rolled samples, while contributing to enhanced strain-hardening rates through increased long-range stress between slip bands.
